did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), to perform skin lesion classification. The methodology involved a two-stage AI approach: first, a U-Net segmentation network was used to detect pigment networks in dermoscopic images, and second, ResNet18 and ResNet50 classifiers were trained to classify skin lesions (melanoma, nevus, etc.) using the segmented pigment networks as an additional input channel. The study utilized datasets from the ISBI 2017 and ISIC 2018 challenges.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a novel two-stage deep learning architecture (U-Net segmentation and ResNet classification) for skin lesion classification. It details specific methodologies, datasets (ISBI 2017, ISIC 2018), implementation details (TensorFlow, image augmentation), and reports quantitative results (recall, F1-score, ROC AUC) from new experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Structure-Aware Convolutional Neural Network for Skin Lesion Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes a structure-aware convolutional neural network for skin lesion classification by integrating prior medical knowledge in the form of pigment network segmentations as an additional input channel. The methodology employs a two-stage approach using a U-Net segmentation network to detect pigment networks and ResNet18 or ResNet50 classifiers for disease diagnosis. The models were trained and evaluated on datasets from the ISBI 2017 and ISIC 2018 challenges, utilizing a disjoint split for segmentation and classification tasks respectively. The main evaluation focused on classification performance metrics, particularly recall and F1-score for malignant melanoma, alongside attention measures to interpret model focus. Key results demonstrated that adding the pigment network channel to the ResNet18 classifier significantly improved the recall for melanoma from 0.213 to 0.400. The authors conclude that incorporating such domain-specific structural information can enhance classifier performance and provide interpretability regarding which features influence the prediction.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary focus of the study; recall improved from 0.213 to 0.400 for ResNet18 with pigment network input. Present in both DS2017 (segmentation dataset) and DS2018 (classification dataset).
Present in both DS2017 (segmentation dataset) and DS2018 (classification dataset). Used for attention analysis alongside melanoma.
Present in DS2017 (segmentation dataset) and DS2018 (classification dataset). Mentioned in related work as a class detected by other methods.
Present in DS2018 (classification dataset). Evaluated via ROC AUC metrics.

all_task_types.target: Discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion).
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on skin lesion classification using ResNet18 and ResNet50 classifiers. The task involves predicting discrete disease labels from dermoscopic images. Performance was evaluated using metrics such as ROC AUC, recall, precision, and F1-score for specific classes like melanoma. Although a U-Net segmentation network was used as a preprocessing step to detect pigment networks, the primary AI task reported for the final diagnostic system is classification.

all_task_types.target: Pigment network structures in dermoscopic images.
all_task_types.details: A U-Net segmentation network was employed to detect pigment networks as a prior knowledge input. This was formulated as a pixel-wise binary segmentation problem (foreground/background) to generate probability maps used as an additional input channel for the classifier.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2017
datasets_used.description: Dataset provided by the ISBI 2017 'Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection' challenge. It contains dermoscopic images with superpixel level annotations of dermoscopic structures, specifically used for training the segmentation network (SN) to detect pigment networks.

use.training: Used to train the U-Net segmentation network (SN) for pigment network detection. The training set was reduced to a subset containing only images with annotated pigment networks.
use.testing: Used to test the segmentation network performance.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: Dataset released for the 'ISIC 2018: Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection' challenge. It comprises dermoscopic images belonging to seven classes of skin lesions. Referenced in the text as HAM10000 in the references.

use.training: Used to train the classifier networks (ResNet18 and ResNet50). The dataset was split into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% testing.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training of the classifier networks.
use.testing: Used for testing the classifier networks to evaluate performance metrics (ROC AUC, recall, F1-score).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used as a loss function for the U-Net segmentation network to handle class imbalance between foreground (pigment network) and background pixels.
Used to evaluate and compare the classification performance of ResNet18 and ResNet50 models with and without the pigment network input channel.
Used to evaluate classification performance, specifically highlighting the improvement in melanoma detection recall when using the pigment network channel with ResNet18.
Used to quantify channel-wise and location-wise attention, measuring the contribution of input pixels to the network's output to interpret model focus.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance improvements were described qualitatively (e.g., 'clear improvement', 'not as accurate but comparable') without statistical significance testing.

DS2017 (ISBI 2017 Challenge Dataset).notes: The U-Net segmentation results are described as 'not as accurate but comparable' to the state-of-the-art results by Kawahara & Hamarneh. The goal was not to perfect the segmentation but to investigate its utility for classification.

DS2018 (ISIC 2018 Challenge Dataset / HA.notes: For ResNet18, adding the pigment network channel improved melanoma recall from 0.213 to 0.400 and F1-score from 0.318 to 0.414, despite a slight decrease in overall ROC AUC. For ResNet50, adding the pigment network channel led to lower ROC AUC, recall, and F1-scores. Channel-wise and location-wise attention measures were higher for ResNet18 than ResNet50, suggesting ResNet50 did not focus as much on the pigment network channel.

architecture_used.main_model: Two-stage system: U-Net segmentation network (SN) followed by ResNet18 or ResNet50 classifier networks (CN).
architecture_used.pretraining: ResNet50 was pre-trained with images from the 2014 ImageNet Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge. ResNet18 was trained from scratch. For the pigment network channel input to ResNet50, only the weights of the first convolutional layer and the final fully-connected layer were learned, while all weights in-between were pre-trained and frozen.

segmentation_network.task: Pixel-wise binary segmentation of pigment networks (foreground/background)

classifier_networks.training_mode: Pre-trained on ImageNet; fine-tuned with frozen intermediate layers for the pigment network channel experiment

architecture_details.input_channels: 4 channels (3 RGB channels + 1 pigment network probability map channel)

training_details.augmentation: Random rotations (90, 180, 270 degrees) and vertical/horizontal flips

classification_or_output_head.classes: 7 classes (melanoma, melanocytic nevus, basal cell carcinoma, actinic keratosis, benign keratosis, dermatofibroma, vascular lesion)

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via random rotations (90, 180, 270 degrees) and vertical/horizontal flips
Segmentation of pigment networks using a U-Net network to generate probability maps
Stacking pigment network probability maps as an additional channel to RGB images (creating 4-channel input)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotations of 90, 180, and 270 degrees applied to both DS2017 and DS2018 datasets.
Vertical and horizontal flips applied to both DS2017 and DS2018 datasets.

conclusions: Incorporating prior medical knowledge in the form of pigment network segmentations as an additional input channel improved the recall for malignant melanoma detection from 0.213 to 0.400 when using a ResNet18 classifier.
The improved melanoma recall achieved by ResNet18 with the pigment network channel was higher than that of the more complex ResNet50 classifier (0.353).

Adding the pigment network channel to a pre-trained ResNet50 classifier resulted in lower ROC AUC, recall, and F1-scores compared to using raw images alone, suggesting that the pre-trained features did not effectively utilize the additional channel without more sophisticated fine-tuning.

Channel-wise and location-wise attention measures were significantly higher for ResNet18 than for ResNet50, indicating that ResNet18 focused more on the medically relevant pigment network structures, whereas ResNet50 did not.

The proposed two-stage approach allows for the integration of domain-specific structural information into deep networks, potentially enabling better performance with fewer parameters and annotations, as well as providing interpretability regarding which features influence the prediction.
Future work could involve integrating other dermoscopic structures, such as streaks and dots, to further improve classifiers, particularly for non-melanocytic lesions.
important_remarks: The segmentation network was trained on a biased subset of images containing only annotated pigment networks, potentially causing the segmenter to always detect a pigment network even when absent.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance improvements were described qualitatively.

Adding the pigment network channel to ResNet50 resulted in decreased performance (lower ROC AUC, recall, and F1-score) compared to using raw images alone, suggesting pre-trained ImageNet features may not effectively utilize additional dermoscopic channels without sophisticated fine-tuning.

ResNet18, trained from scratch, showed higher channel-wise and location-wise attention to the pigment network than ResNet50, indicating better focus on medically relevant structures despite lower overall complexity.
The study relies on disjoint datasets for segmentation (ISBI 2017) and classification (ISIC 2018/HAM10000), limiting the ability to evaluate end-to-end learning on a single unified distribution.

The segmentation performance (U-Net) was comparable but not superior to state-of-the-art methods (Kawahara & Hamarneh), indicating the approach prioritizes classification utility over segmentation perfection.
The method was evaluated only on dermoscopic images from public challenge datasets; external validation on independent clinical datasets was not reported.

Interpretability was assessed using attribution methods (input × partial derivative), but the paper notes that saliency maps in prior work provided limited interpretable information compared to their channel-wise attention measures.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized two disjoint datasets: DS2017 (ISBI 2017 challenge dataset) for training and testing a U-Net segmentation network to detect pigment networks, and DS2018 (ISIC 2018 challenge dataset/HAM10000) for training, validating, and testing ResNet18 and ResNet50 classifiers. The segmentation network was trained on a biased subset of DS2017 containing only images with annotated pigment networks. The classifiers were trained on DS2018 using an 80/10/10 split. No external cross-dataset validation was performed; the study highlights the limitation of using disjoint datasets for segmentation and classification tasks.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper explicitly states that the datasets for segmentation (DS2017) and classification (DS2018) are disjoint. The models were not trained on one and tested on the other in a cross-dataset validation manner. The segmentation model was tested on DS2017, and the classification models were tested on the held-out test set of DS2018.

training.counts: 2000 images (subset with annotated pigment networks used for U-Net training)
training.notes: Used to train the U-Net segmentation network (SN). The training set was reduced to a subset containing only images where a pigment network was annotated, creating a biased segmenter.
Used to train the classifier networks (ResNet18 and ResNet50). ResNet50 was pre-trained on ImageNet, but fine-tuned on this dataset.

validation.notes: Used for model selection and hyperparameter tuning during the training of the classifier networks.

testing.notes: Used to test the performance of the U-Net segmentation network.
Used for final evaluation of the classifier networks (ResNet18 and ResNet50) with and without the pigment network channel.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.dataset: ImageNet (2014 Large Scale Visual Recognition Challenge)
pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: ResNet50 was pre-trained on ImageNet images. The weights from the intermediate layers were frozen, and only the first convolutional layer and final fully-connected layer were learned during fine-tuning on DS2018. ResNet18 was trained from scratch.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Data augmentation was performed using random rotations (90, 180, 270 degrees) and vertical/horizontal flips on both datasets.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: DS2017: 2000 training, 600 testing. DS2018: 80% training (8012), 10% validation (1001), 10% testing (1002).
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No external unseen dataset was used for testing. The test set for classification was a held-out portion of the same DS2018 dataset used for training.

other_metrics.dice_coefficient: Used as loss function, value not reported in results table

dataset_level_results.notes: Segmentation results compared against Kawahara and Hamarneh baseline. U-Net performance was 'not as accurate but comparable'.
Baseline ResNet18 performance on DS2018 test set.
Adding pigment network channel improved melanoma recall from 0.213 to 0.400.
Baseline ResNet50 performance on DS2018 test set.
Adding pigment network channel to pre-trained ResNet50 decreased performance compared to raw images.

generalizability_notes: The study relies on disjoint datasets for segmentation (ISBI 2017) and classification (ISIC 2018/HAM10000), limiting the ability to evaluate end-to-end learning on a single unified distribution.
The segmentation network was trained on a biased subset of images containing only annotated pigment networks, potentially causing the segmenter to always detect a pigment network even when absent.
The method was evaluated only on dermoscopic images from public challenge datasets; external validation on independent clinical datasets was not reported.

Adding the pigment network channel to ResNet50 resulted in decreased performance, suggesting pre-trained ImageNet features may not effectively utilize additional dermoscopic channels without sophisticated fine-tuning.
information_not_reported: No external test set results were reported.
No statistical significance testing (p-values) was performed.
Patient-level separation details were not explicitly reported, though image-level splits were used.
